title: Possessives
intro: Possessives show who something belongs to — my, your, his, her, our. In Spanish, they agree with the THING owned (not the owner).

sections.title: Key rule — agree with the THING, not the owner
sections.content:
Spanish possessives agree with what is owned, not who owns it:
• mi libro (my book) — libro is masculine → mi (not *mio)
• mi casa (my house) — casa is feminine → mi (same word!)
• But: nuestro libro / nuestra casa — nuestro changes for gender

'mi' and 'tu' don't change for gender. 'nuestro' and 'vuestro' do!

sections.title: 'su' can mean many things
sections.content:
'su / sus' covers: his, her, your (formal), their, your (plural)
Context usually makes it clear:
• Carlos habla con su madre. (Carlos speaks with his mother.)
• Usted habla con su jefe. (You speak with your boss.)

sections.label: 🌍 Spain vs Latin America
sections.content:
In Spain, 'vuestro/a/os/as' is used for 'your' (plural informal).
In Latin America, 'su/sus' is used for ALL plural 'your' (formal and informal).
Both forms are understood everywhere.

questions.explanation: 'my' before a singular noun = mi (no gender change!) ✓
casa is feminine → nuestra casa ✓ (nuestro/nuestra agrees with the noun!)
'your' (informal singular) = tu ✓ (no accent — it's a possessive, not 'tú' = you)
casas is feminine plural → nuestras casas ✓
